mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 4 Series 430d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 430d engine typically costs £4,000–£9,500 supply only depending on generation, with the current B57D30 sitting at £4,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 4 Series 430d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on which generation you're asking about. The N57 (F32, 2013–2020) is more reliable than the problematic N47, but timing chain issues (though rarer) and bottom-end bearing failures have been reported. The B57 (G22, 2020+) is generally improved with mild-hybrid tech, but oil leaks and AdBlue/EGR system issues are reported. Across both, oil-change discipline is everything.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 430d?

acceptedAnswer.text: N57 timing chain wear and bottom-end bearing failure (F32), EGR cooler failure (both generations — recall item), B57 oil leaks from valve cover and oil filter housing (G22), and AdBlue/SCR system issues (G22). See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 430d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £10,000–£15,000 F32 430d, a £4,500–£5,500 recon is 30–50% of the car's value — often worth it for a good example. Against a £25,000+ G22, a £6,000 recon is 25% — worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 430d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: F32/F33/F36 (2013–2020) = N57D30; G22/G23/G26 (2020–present) = B57D30. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Has the BMW 430d been recalled for the EGR cooler issue?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — BMW has extended its diesel car recall multiple times due to concerns that a faulty EGR cooler part presents a fire risk. Glycol leakage from the EGR cooler can occur. Check your vehicle's recall status immediately — if your 430d hasn't had the updated EGR cooler fitted, get it done. This is a safety-critical item.

problems.title: N57 Timing Chain & Guide Wear (F32, all years)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine on cold start, metallic clattering, engine warning light, loss of power, potential engine failure if chain skips or snaps.
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if chain snaps and valves meet pistons)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: While N57 timing chain failures are rarer than the notorious N47 issues, they do occur. The problem stems from chain stretch and wear on the plastic guides. A cold-start rattle is the early warning sign — ignore it and you risk piston-valve contact and a full engine replacement. Regular oil changes with the correct BMW-approved oil are the best prevention — many specialists recommend 8,000-mile intervals rather than BMW's 18,000-mile CBS schedule. At the first sign of rattle, get it inspected immediately.

problems.title: N57 Bottom-End Bearing Failure (F32)
problems.symptoms: Knocking noise from the bottom of the engine, especially under load, low oil pressure warning, metallic debris in oil filter, eventual engine seizure.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear knocking, stop driving immediately

problems.recommendation: N57 engines can suffer from bottom-end bearing failure, often linked to oil starvation or oil degradation. Extended oil change intervals are a contributing factor. If you hear a knocking noise from the bottom end, stop driving immediately and have it inspected. Regular oil changes with BMW-approved oil are the key prevention — many specialists recommend 8,000-mile intervals.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Failure (N57 & B57 — Recall Item)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, occasional burning smell, engine warning light, potential fire risk.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — subject to BMW recalls
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (unless coolant loss leads to overheating damage)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: check recall status immediately; can cause fire risk

problems.recommendation: EGR cooler failure is a well-documented issue across N57 and B57 engines. BMW has extended its diesel car recall multiple times due to concerns that a faulty part presents a fire risk. Glycol leakage from the EGR cooler can occur, and in some cases, can create a fire risk. Check your vehicle's recall status immediately — if your 430d hasn't had the updated EGR cooler fitted, get it done. This is a safety-critical item.

problems.title: B57 Oil Leaks (G22 — Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, low oil level warning, visible oil seepage around the valve cover or oil filter housing.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,000 (valve cover gasket or oil filter housing gasket replacement)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon; oil leaks can damage other components

problems.recommendation: The B57 engine has a well-documented tendency for oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing. The rubber seals harden due to heat and age, causing oil to leak. Leaking oil can saturate and degrade vacuum, coolant, and air intake hoses, and can foul other components. Regular inspections can catch this before it becomes a major issue. If you notice oil spots under your car or a burning oil smell, have it inspected promptly.

problems.title: AdBlue / SCR System Issues (B57 — G22)
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, AdBlue countdown timer starting, reduced power, emissions warning, MOT failure.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,200 (sensor replacement, AdBlue system service, or crystallisation removal)【PLACEHOLDER】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if AdBlue countdown appears

problems.recommendation: The B57's AdBlue (SCR) system is essential for meeting Euro 6d emissions standards. Like many modern diesels, AdBlue crystallisation in the injector or exhaust pipe, NOx sensor failures, and heater unit faults are reported. If the countdown timer starts, do not ignore it — once it reaches zero, the engine will not restart. Regular AdBlue system maintenance and using high-quality AdBlue reduces risk. This is a common issue on B57-powered BMWs.

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 430d? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with oil changes at 8,000–10,000 mile intervals (not BMW's 18,000-mile CBS schedule)
* EGR cooler recall completed (check with BMW)
* N57 cars (2013–2020): no cold-start rattle from the engine
* N57 cars (2013–2020): no knocking from the bottom end
* B57 cars (2020+): no oil leaks and AdBlue system functioning correctly
avoidIf: * No proof of regular oil changes — these engines are unforgiving of neglect
* N57 with cold-start rattle — timing chain work is imminent and expensive
* N57 with knocking from the bottom end — bearing failure is likely
* EGR cooler not replaced — potential fire risk
* Any engine warning light without diagnosis — the N57 and B57 can hide expensive faults

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The BMW 430d is a genuinely special car — a sleek, stylish coupé with a silky-smooth straight-six diesel that delivers effortless performance and remarkable fuel economy. The N57-powered F32 generation offers proven performance but demands timing chain and bottom-end vigilance, along with EGR cooler recall completion. The B57-powered G22 generation is a significant step forward in refinement and reliability, with the added benefit of mild-hybrid efficiency. Across both generations, one rule applies: oil change discipline is everything. BMW's extended CBS servicing is widely considered inadequate — treat your 430d to 8,000–10,000 mile oil changes and it will reward you with hundreds of thousands of miles of effortless motoring.

cta.label: → Compare 430d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 430d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle from the engine (N57 timing chain warning)
* Service History — Including which engine era (N57/B57) and confirmed oil-change discipline (8k–10k miles max)
* EGR Cooler — Check recall status; this is a safety-critical item on N57 and B57
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve cover and oil filter housing (B57)
* Bottom End — Any knocking from the bottom of the engine? (N57 bearing warning)
* AdBlue System — Any warnings or countdowns? (B57)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Timing, EGR, oil, or AdBlue codes are most common
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (EGR, DPF, NOx sensor issues)

items.label: SECTION 8 — FAQ
items.value: 1. How much does a BMW 4 Series 430d engine replacement cost in the UK?

items.label: A reconditioned 430d engine typically costs £4,000–£9,500 supply only depending on generation, with the current B57D30 sitting at £4,500–£9,500. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.
items.value: 2. Is the BMW 4 Series 430d engine reliable?

items.label: It depends on which generation you're asking about. The N57 (F32, 2013–2020) is more reliable than the problematic N47, but timing chain issues (though rarer) and bottom-end bearing failures have been reported. The B57 (G22, 2020+) is generally improved — more refined and efficient with mild-hybrid tech — but oil leaks and AdBlue/EGR system issues are reported. Across both, oil-change discipline is everything.
items.value: 3. What are the common problems with the BMW 430d?

items.label: N57 timing chain wear and bottom-end bearing failure (F32), EGR cooler failure (both generations — recall item), B57 oil leaks from valve cover and oil filter housing (G22), and AdBlue/SCR system issues (G22). See the Era Map above for generation-specific advice.
items.value: 4. Is it worth replacing a BMW 430d engine, or should I scrap the car?

items.label: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £10,000–£15,000 F32 430d, a £4,500–£5,500 recon is 30–50% of the car's value — often worth it for a good example. Against a £25,000+ G22, a £6,000 recon is 25% — worth it. The BMW scrap threshold of 70% means evaluating the whole vehicle and its condition before committing.
items.value: 5. How do I find my BMW 430d engine code?

items.label: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. As a rule of thumb: F32/F33/F36 (2013–2020) = N57D30; G22/G23/G26 (2020–present) = B57D30. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.
items.value: 6. Has the BMW 430d been recalled for the EGR cooler issue?

items.label: Yes — BMW has extended its diesel car recall multiple times due to concerns that a faulty EGR cooler part presents a fire risk. Glycol leakage from the EGR cooler can occur. Check your vehicle's recall status immediately — if your 430d hasn't had the updated EGR cooler fitted, get it done. This is a safety-critical item.
items.value: Disclaimer: All prices are indicative UK market ranges based on historical quote data from our network of engine specialists. Actual costs vary by variant, engine code, damage severity and workshop location. Use our free comparison tool to get tailored quotes. Every engine supplied through EnginesMarket carries a minimum 12-month unlimited-mileage warranty — always confirm terms with your chosen supplier.
